title: STUDY TOPIC: - Nativity part 14
scripture_text: ‘’In those days Caesar Augustus issued a decree that a census should be taken of the entire Roman world. (This was the first census that took place while Quirinius was governor of Syria.) And everyone went to their own town to register. So Joseph also went up from the town of Nazareth in Galilee to Judea, to Bethlehem the town of David, because he belonged to the house and line of David. He went there to register with Mary, who was pledged to be married to him and was expecting a child. While they were there, the time came for the baby to be born, and she gave birth to her firstborn, a son. She wrapped him in cloths and placed him in a manger, because there was no guest room available for them.’’
devotional:
Some version translate verse 7 as ‘No room in the Inn.

So we need to ask, ‘How should all this affect my life?’

1. The incarnation should radically affect our family lives.

Think about it this way, the Jesus that is born in the stable is an excluded Jesus, he’s ‘out there,’he is not invading our space.’

And let us be honest, that is the kind of Jesus many people want. But the Jesus that is born in the ‘Katalyma.’ guest room, well he becomes a guest. We are so glad that he comes and stays for a while, we make a special effort to accommodate him, we have time when we go out of our way to fit our lives around him. However, treating him as a guest means that at its essence the majority of our lives is not really about him. Being a guest in someone’s home is lovely but it is never the same as being part of the family, a guest will never have the same freedom as a member of the family.

I think it is so easy for anyone of us to put Jesus into the stable or the guest room in order to protect and control our own space. One of the first lessons we learn from the incarnation of Jesus is that he wants to sanctify the ordinariness of family life. The last place he wants to be is in a stable or a guest room, rather he wants to be, [and we need him to be] at the very heart of family life.

Let us take a step back and think about the implications of what this means.

a. Is our family life centred around Jesus?

This is not an easy question to hear or to answer. Having a Christ centred family will only happen if it is intentional, it will never happen by default? How do we know if we have Christ centred family? One of the ways to answer that question is to ask ourselves if our lives as a family would be very different if we had no knowledge of Jesus or his presence. In other words, would/could our family life survive with little or no acknowledgement of Jesus? Has the reality of Jesus at the heart of our home become an absolute essential for our families and marriages.

b. Are our families a place where there is space for the life of Jesus to grow? Jesus was born into the heart if the family, not in the stable or the guest room, and it was in the context of the family that Jesus grew. As parents, Mary and Joseph were continually learning and discovering new things about Jesus. I like that. As families we need a corporate experience of discovering Jesus. I think at times we have over individualised our relationship with Jesus.

We have heard of married couples who are happy to do their own thing. He does his thing with his mates, and she does her thing with her friends, and occasionally they do something together. The reason I mentioned this, is because for the most part when it comes to our spiritual lives, there can be an over independence. We are like the above mentioned couple. We do our own ‘spiritual’ thing during the week. Then we go to church together on a Sunday. A Christian family which is Christ centred, is about having a shared experience of Jesus in the context of our homes. Just let me say that being a church going family is very different than being a Christian family.

c. Are our families a place that is dominated by love? Could you say that love is the main thing that continually characterises you family life?

There is something fascinating about this passage that is very easily missed. Look how Luke describes Mary, ‘He went there to register with Mary, who was pledged to be married to him.’ He does not call Mary his wife, they were not married at this time. We know from Matthew 1:25. ‘But he did not consummate their marriage until she gave birth to a son. This is important because consummation was part of the wedding celebration, and Joseph did not want to be identified as Jesus’ biological father, so they probably got married after Jesus birth.

So think how radical Joseph’s love for Mary must have been. To completely accept her, never ashamed to be seen with her. His acceptance of Mary was deeply embedded in the reality of the life of Jesus. An authentic Christian home is where people are accepted because of the life of Jesus. Sadly in many homes that claim to be Christian, there is control, not acceptance, manipulation, not acceptance, a desire to change others not acceptance, resentment not acceptance. There are homes were the experience of love is the unusual rather than normal, where being served is more important than serving. And we have excluded Jesus to either the stable or the guest room. When our lives as families is defined by Jesus and his love, then we will be better resourced to deal with the inevitability of tension, stress and pain.

A Christian author, Kaylene Yoder wrote, ‘The condition of our hearts will shape the culture of our home.’

4. If the authentic life of Jesus is the essence of our homes it will affect those outside our home.

As we have said there was nothing miraculous about Jesus’ birth.(His conception was of course miraculous!) But His birth was a very ordinary birth. Mary had to go through all the usual labour pains in order to bring Jesus into the world. But what was miraculous was what happened in the fields of Bethlehem. Angels appeared to despised shepherds and they were drawn to Jesus.

There is a story in Mark 7 where Jesus wanted some time to himself verse 24 says.

‘He got up and departed from there to the region of Tyre. He entered a house and did not want anyone to know it, but he could not escape notice.’ Christian Standard Bible.

I love that. It was Jesus ‘In the house that could not escape notice,’

When the life of Jesus fills our homes the angels have something to sing about and the world becomes a better place. There is something very powerful and relevant about a home that pulsates with the life of Jesus.

Jesus once said, “Anyone who loves me will obey my teaching. My Father will love them, and we will come to them and make our home with them.’ John 14:23.

Jesus is not looking for a stable or a guest room, but a home. I want you to think about the culture of your family, is it Christ centred and Christ orientated?

Are their strong tangible evidences that the life of Jesus is transforming the dynamics within your family life. It is interesting that the Incarnation of Jesus was first displayed through a family.

Let us take Jesus out of the stable so he can be at home with us.
prayer: Father forgive me for wanting to keep Jesus in a stable.
html:
STUDY TOPIC: - Nativity part 14

Study Text: The birth of Jesus Luke 2:1-7
‘’In those days Caesar Augustus issued a decree that a census should be taken of the entire Roman world. (This was the first census that took place while Quirinius was governor of Syria.) And everyone went to their own town to register. So Joseph also went up from the town of Nazareth in Galilee to Judea, to Bethlehem the town of David, because he belonged to the house and line of David. He went there to register with Mary, who was pledged to be married to him and was expecting a child. While they were there, the time came for the baby to be born, and she gave birth to her firstborn, a son. She wrapped him in cloths and placed him in a manger, because there was no guest room available for them.’’

Some version translate verse 7 as ‘No room in the Inn.

So we need to ask, ‘How should all this affect my life?’

1. The incarnation should radically affect our family lives.

Think about it this way, the Jesus that is born in the stable is an excluded Jesus, he’s ‘out there,’he is not invading our space.’

Just let me say that being a church going family is very different than being a Christian family.

We know from Matthew 1:25. ‘But he did not consummate their marriage until she gave birth to a son. Sadly in many homes that claim to be Christian, there is control, not acceptance, manipulation, not acceptance, a desire to change others not acceptance, resentment not acceptance. There are homes were the experience of love is the unusual rather than normal, where being served is more important than serving. And we have excluded Jesus to either the stable or the guest room. 4. If the authentic life of Jesus is the essence of our homes it will affect those outside our home.

As we have said there was nothing miraculous about Jesus’ birth.(His conception was of course miraculous!) But His birth was a very ordinary birth. Mary had to go through all the usual labour pains in order to bring Jesus into the world. But what was miraculous was what happened in the fields of Bethlehem. Angels appeared to despised shepherds and they were drawn to Jesus.

There is a story in Mark 7 where Jesus wanted some time to himself verse 24 says.

‘He got up and departed from there to the region of Tyre. He entered a house and did not want anyone to know it, but he could not escape notice.’ Christian Standard Bible.

I love that. It was Jesus ‘In the house that could not escape notice,’

When the life of Jesus fills our homes the angels have something to sing about and the world becomes a better place. There is something very powerful and relevant about a home that pulsates with the life of Jesus.

Jesus once said, “Anyone who loves me will obey my teaching. My Father will love them, and we will come to them and make our home with them.’ John 14:23.

Jesus is not looking for a stable or a guest room, but a home. I want you to think about the culture of your family, is it Christ centred and Christ orientated?

Are their strong tangible evidences that the life of Jesus is transforming the dynamics within your family life. It is interesting that the Incarnation of Jesus was first displayed through a family.

Let us take Jesus out of the stable so he can be at home with us.

Prayer: Father forgive me for wanting to keep Jesus in a stable.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Esther 1-3
